prompt_md: Which `#include` form should you use when including a header from your own project?

options.c: `#include myheader.h` — no brackets needed for local files
options.d: Either form works identically; it is a matter of style

explanation_md: Double quotes tell the compiler to search relative to the current file first, which is correct for project-local headers. Angle brackets search system/standard paths and are reserved for standard library and third-party headers. The two forms are NOT interchangeable — using angle brackets for a local header can fail to find it.
`triple(4)` returns `4 * 3 = 12`. The header provides the declaration so `main.cpp` compiles; the linker finds the definition in `util.cpp` and links everything together.

options.a: Everything works fine — the compiler deduplicates identical definitions
options.b: The compiler errors on the second `#include` because the function is already defined
options.c: The linker errors with a multiple-definition error
options.d: The program compiles and links but produces wrong output at runtime

explanation_md: Each `.cpp` file that includes the header gets its own compiled definition of `area`. When the linker tries to combine them, it sees two definitions of the same symbol and refuses with a 'multiple definition' error. The fix is to keep only the declaration (`int area(int w, int h);`) in the header and move the body to a `.cpp` file.

options.a: It is required by the compiler; without it the file will not compile
options.b: It lets the compiler verify the definition matches the declaration, catching mismatches early
options.c: It prevents the linker from seeing the function at all
options.d: It is optional and only done for documentation purposes

explanation_md: Including the own header is not strictly required for compilation, but it is a vital practice. When the `.cpp` includes its header, the compiler cross-checks each definition against the corresponding declaration. If a parameter type or return type was changed in one place but not the other, the compiler reports an error immediately rather than allowing a silent mismatch.

A forward declaration followed by a full definition in the same file is perfectly valid. The declaration and definition both refer to `double_it(int)`, so the compiler is happy. `double_it(5)` returns `10`, which is printed followed by a newline.
